quote: My name is Corey. I'm with Ion. I was just reaching out because I got a request that you were looking at solar for your home.
stewart_read: Corey's intro uses vague 'I got a request' framing instead of the schema-required 'the solar form you filled out online.' The distinction matters: 'I got a request' sounds like a cold call; 'the form you filled out' anchors the call as customer-initiated. Richard's immediate legitimacy probe ('are you tied with Pacific Power or is what's the deal?') is a direct consequence of this framing gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey may have been uncertain which form or lead source generated this contact and chose vague language to avoid a mismatch. Rejected — the schema's standard intro is designed precisely for this situation and the vague framing demonstrably triggered the legitimacy probe.
coaching_implication: Open the 1-on-1 here. Ask Corey: 'What does the schema say about how to reference the lead source in the intro, and why does it matter?' The answer is in intros.standard. The legitimacy probe at 00:59 is the direct downstream cost of skipping it.

quote: I I don't know where you're calling from, but I live in Astoria, and it's overcast most of the time. So Yeah. So because no one has solar here.
stewart_read: Richard raises a geographic objection — Astoria's overcast climate — that is a real, addressable concern. Corey's response (02:15) is to repeat the bill-swap description rather than engage with the objection or redirect to the specialist. The overcast concern is a legitimate qualifier question that a specialist could address with actual data; Corey neither validates it nor bridges to the specialist's expertise. This is a knowledge gap compounded by a missed curiosity-creation opportunity.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey may have judged that engaging the overcast objection would open a technical debate he couldn't win and chose to stay on the bill-swap track. Partially supported — but the correct move is still to acknowledge the concern and bridge to the specialist, not to ignore it entirely.
coaching_implication: Coach Corey on the universal escape hatch from coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — they'll look at your specific roof and location. Let me get you on the calendar.' The overcast objection is not a DQ; it's a specialist question. Corey needs to hear the distinction.

quote: So with with Solar, you gotta realize it's, like, by system sizes, not by because we're not charging you per kilowatt that you use.
stewart_read: Corey is now explaining solar pricing mechanics — system-size billing vs. per-kilowatt billing — in response to Richard's 'how do you make money' question. This is closer-tier content. Verify has not been completed. Bill pain has not been anchored. The rep has been pulled entirely off the setter's structural path by a customer question he should have bridged to the specialist. The explanation is also incomplete (it trails off mid-sentence), which compounds the confusion rather than resolving it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey may have judged that Richard needed a basic conceptual frame before he'd agree to an appointment. Rejected — the explanation didn't land (Richard asked the same question again at 03:43), and the schema's escape hatch would have served better here.
coaching_implication: This is the clearest scope-creep moment on the call. Show Corey the setter_scope_discipline escape hatch and ask him to role-play the 'how do you make money' question using it. The goal is to make the bridge reflex automatic before the next call.

quote: So at this point in time, we don't pay anything. And the solar But
stewart_read: Richard is trailing off toward a question about what happens after the system is paid off — a genuine curiosity signal buried in a confused sentence. More importantly, his framing ('we don't pay anything') is a flip opportunity per analysis_directives.flip_opportunity_detection: the 'we pay nothing' frame could be redirected to 'right now you're paying Pacific Power every month — the question is whether that payment goes to them or to a system you own.' Corey's response (04:28) pivots to scope-discipline transparency ('I'm only in the scheduling department'), which is the correct move for the question Richard was building toward — but the flip opportunity in Richard's own framing goes untouched. Note: Richard's use of 'we' here is a possible signal that a household member is involved in this decision, but the transcript contains no other evidence of a co-decision-maker. Worth the closer noting, not a confirmed dynamic.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey's scope-discipline pivot at 04:28 was intentional — he recognized he was in over his head and correctly deferred to the specialist. Supported. But the flip opportunity in Richard's 'we don't pay anything' framing was available before the pivot and was missed entirely.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to introduce objection_inversion to Corey. The flip doesn't require knowing the exact numbers — it's an emotional redirect: 'You're already paying for power every month. The question is who gets that payment.' Flag that this leverage point would land harder if Corey knew Richard's actual Pacific Power bill — which is exactly why verify and bill_anchor come before this conversation.

quote: I I think you're gonna end up, wasting your time with me. So you you might wanna move on to your next name so that you can, you know, get somebody that might need might actually go through with it because it's unlikely we we would.
stewart_read: Richard's guilt-as-objection framing ('you're wasting your time') is a soft refusal disguised as consideration for Corey. The correct reframe is to return control to Richard — something like: 'You filled out the form, which means you had a reason. I'm not here to pressure you — I just want to make sure you have the actual numbers before you decide.' Corey's response (05:05) goes to the free-estimate pitch, which is directionally correct (rebuttals.changed_mind: 'create curiosity, don't argue') but misses the emotional reframe of Richard's guilt framing. The changed_mind rebuttal is the closest schema analog, but the guilt-as-objection pattern — where the customer frames exit as a favor to the rep — is a distinct dynamic not yet formally defined in the schema. The coaching gap here is real but the schema reference is approximate.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey's free-estimate pitch was a deliberate attempt to lower the stakes and keep Richard on the line. Partially supported — the instinct is right. But the execution missed the emotional reframe that would have made it land.
coaching_implication: This is a TBD coaching gap in the schema (guilt-as-objection reframe is not yet formally defined). Flag for Kenny to add. In the 1-on-1, coach Corey on the distinction: the changed_mind rebuttal handles 'I'm not interested'; the guilt-as-objection reframe handles 'you should move on.' They require different responses. A suggested reframe direction (not verbatim): return control to Richard by referencing his own form submission as evidence of his original interest.

quote: I also won't have any of my specialists come out to you in person or anything like that with any high pressure sales pitch. I'll just have one of my specialists give you a phone call, email you the report, answer any questions you have.
stewart_read: Corey is making promises about the specialist's process — phone call format, email delivery of the report — that he has no authority to make. This is a scope-creep deviation: the setter's job is to schedule the appointment, not to describe how the specialist will conduct it. 'Email you the report' in particular is a promise that may conflict with the closer's actual process and could create a trust gap if the specialist doesn't deliver it that way. This is not a rebuttals.send_proposal_before_meeting violation (Richard never asked for the proposal in advance); it's an unauthorized description of the appointment format.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey was trying to reduce Richard's anxiety about a high-pressure in-person visit, which is a legitimate concern to address. Supported as intent — but the execution overreaches the setter's authority and makes promises the closer may not keep.
coaching_implication: Show Corey the button_up protocol and setter_scope_discipline escape hatch. The anxiety about in-person pressure is real and worth addressing — but the correct move is: 'It's a phone call, no one comes to your door, and if you don't like the numbers you just say so.' That addresses the anxiety without promising specifics about the specialist's process that Corey can't control.

quote: Yeah. Yeah. It it never turns into free.
stewart_read: This is Richard's clearest articulation of hidden-costs anxiety — a direct challenge to Corey's 'free estimate' framing. Corey's response ('I trust you. It well, yeah, it's not well, it is a free estimate, but nothing else is required of you') is halting and partially concedes Richard's point before recovering. The hesitation ('I trust you. It well, yeah...') signals that Corey doesn't have a clean answer to this objection. A hidden-costs rebuttal is listed as TBD in the schema (rebuttals section, TBD note referencing SESSION20). This is a real gap: the objection is common, the schema doesn't have a canonical response, and Corey's improvised answer didn't hold.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Corey's halting response was an attempt to validate Richard's skepticism before reasserting the free-estimate framing. Rejected — the hesitation reads as uncertainty, not validation, and Richard's subsequent hard exit confirms it didn't land.
coaching_implication: Flag this for Kenny as a schema gap: hidden_costs_anxiety rebuttal is TBD and this call shows why it matters. In the 1-on-1, coach Corey that the correct move is to validate the skepticism directly ('You're right that solar isn't free — the system has a cost') and then bridge to the bill-swap frame ('the question is whether that cost is less than what you're paying Pacific Power now'). Don't fabricate numbers; the specialist has them. This is a flip opportunity, not a reassurance play.
